prior.title: Decarbonising Transport: A Better, Greener Britain - UK Government Policy Framework
prior.summary: UK government directive outlining comprehensive transport decarbonization strategy to achieve net-zero emissions through vehicle electrification, infrastructure development, and modal shift policies.
prior.full_brief:
# Decarbonising Transport: A Better, Greener Britain — UK Government Policy Framework

## Market Signal Brief

---

# Purpose and Scope of This Document

This brief analyses the UK Government's *Decarbonising Transport: A Better, Greener Britain* Transport Decarbonisation Plan (TDP), published July 2021, as a policy framework signal with active delivery vehicles — notably the Zero Emission HGV and Infrastructure Demonstrator (ZEHID) programme — that create near-term operational and procurement implications. The brief is anchored to workspaces operating road freight as a primary or secondary transport mode, with cargo verticals including live events, fine art, film and TV, high-value automotive, and humanitarian freight, and acting as freight forwarder, importer, or fulfillment provider across UK trade lanes.

Convention notes: "the workspace" refers to the freight-forwarding operation throughout. Severity label MONITORING applies: no immediate compliance action is mandated by the TDP itself (it is a policy framework, not enacted regulation), but the delivery programmes it underpins — including ZEV mandates and HGV diesel bans — create procurement and fleet-planning windows that require active positioning now. Re-check window: Q4 2026 (end of ZEHID Phase 1 infrastructure build and publication of interim Ricardo evaluation findings).

---

# What's Moving and What Triggered It

MONITORING

**The framework.** The UK Department for Transport published *Decarbonising transport: a better, greener Britain* on 14 July 2021, setting out "the government's commitments and the actions needed to decarbonise the entire transport system in the UK." *Source: Transport Decarbonisation Plan, Department for Transport, 14 July 2021. https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/transport-decarbonisation-plan.

The TDP followed *Decarbonising transport: setting the challenge*, published in March 2020, "which laid out the scale of additional reductions needed to deliver transport's contribution to legally binding carbon budgets and delivering net zero by 2050." *Source: Transport Decarbonisation Plan, Department for Transport, 14 July 2021. https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/transport-decarbonisation-plan.

A one-year-on review was published in July 2022, "which gave a summary of our progress over the 12 months since *Decarbonising transport: a better, greener Britain* was published and outlined upcoming milestones." *Source: Transport Decarbonisation Plan, Department for Transport, 14 July 2021 (updated 12 January 2023). https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/transport-decarbonisation-plan.

**What triggered continued urgency.** The Climate Change Committee's (CCC) 2025 Progress Report to Parliament, published 25 June 2025, found that "surface transport alone contributes almost 30% of the emissions reduction required during this period" (to the 2030 Nationally Determined Contribution). *Source: Progress in Reducing Emissions — 2025 Report to Parliament, Climate Change Committee, 25 June 2025. https://www.theccc.org.uk/publication/progress-in-reducing-emissions-2025-report-to-parliament.

The CCC also noted that "over 80% of the required emissions savings between now and 2030 need to come from sectors other than energy supply," with surface transport identified as a primary contributor. *Source: Progress in Reducing Emissions — 2025 Report to Parliament, Climate Change Committee, 25 June 2025. https://www.theccc.org.uk/publication/progress-in-reducing-emissions-2025-report-to-parliament.

**The signal on aviation.** The 2025 CCC report noted that "as a result of this increase [in emissions from flying], aviation now contributes a greater share of total UK emissions than the entire electricity supply sector" and that "continued emissions growth in this sector could put future targets at risk." *Source: Progress in Reducing Emissions — 2025 Report to Parliament, Climate Change Committee, 25 June 2025. https://www.theccc.org.uk/publication/progress-in-reducing-emissions-2025-report-to-parliament.

*Operational implication:* For workspaces prioritising air freight as their primary mode, the growing regulatory scrutiny of aviation emissions in the UK context — explicitly flagged by the CCC as a sector now exceeding electricity supply in its emissions share — raises the probability that aviation-specific compliance obligations will intensify beyond the TDP's current aviation framing. This is a forward signal, not a current obligation.

**HGV diesel ban commitments.** The TDP includes "measures to ban the sale of new large diesel trucks weighing more than 26 tonnes by 2040, with smaller diesel trucks banned from 2035." *Source: CCC responds to Government's Transport Decarbonisation Plan, Climate Change Committee, 14 July 2021. https://www.theccc.org.uk/2021/07/14/ccc-responds-to-governments-transport-decarbonisation-plan/.

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces contracting third-party road haulage for cargo movements across UK lanes — live events equipment, automotive parts, high-value film and TV freight — the 2035 cut-off for smaller diesel HGVs represents the outer boundary of current-generation diesel fleet contracts with road carriers. Fleet-renewal timelines for carriers will begin to materialise as procurement decisions within the next three to five years, and any multi-year haulage contract extending toward 2035 should be assessed for transition risk.

**ZEV mandate.** The TDP included "proposals for a Zero Emission Vehicle mandate — which would see car makers having to produce a minimum number of electric vehicles for sale." The CCC described this as "key to reducing emissions from UK transport since the Net Zero target was set." *Source: CCC responds to Government's Transport Decarbonisation Plan, Climate Change Committee, 14 July 2021. https://www.theccc.org.uk/2021/07/14/ccc-responds-to-governments-transport-decarbonisation-plan/.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* The ZEV mandate as it applies to commercial vehicle manufacturers and its specific thresholds, timelines, and compliance obligations for fleet operators or freight forwarders acting as intermediaries requires legal review against the enacted ZEV mandate regulations rather than the TDP's consultation text. The TDP consultation language cited above is not the final legal instrument.

**EV market trajectory.** The CCC's 2025 report found that "the number of electric cars on the road [is] doubling roughly every two years" and that "price parity with petrol cars has already been reached in parts of the second-hand market." *Source: Progress in Reducing Emissions — 2025 Report to Parliament, Climate Change Committee, 25 June 2025. https://www.theccc.org.uk/publication/progress-in-reducing-emissions-2025-report-to-parliament.

*Analytical inference:* The acceleration in EV adoption — now confirmed by the statutory advisor at doubling rates — means that the road freight ecosystem the workspace depends on for final-mile delivery of sensitive cargo (fine art, live events, film and TV equipment) will face carrier fleet transition pressure earlier than the headline diesel ban dates imply. Carriers facing capital redeployment at scale may pass cost through surcharges or restructure service offerings, creating mid-contract pricing risk.

---

# Who's Driving It and What They Want

The Department for Transport (DfT) is the primary policy author. The TDP represents "the government's commitments and the actions needed to decarbonise the entire transport system in the UK," covering the pathway to net zero transport, wider co-benefits, and the principles underpinning delivery. *Source: Transport Decarbonisation Plan, Department for Transport, 14 July 2021. https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/transport-decarbonisation-plan.

The Climate Change Committee acts as the statutory advisory and scrutiny body. Its initial TDP response confirmed alignment with its recommendations on ZEV mandates and diesel truck phase-outs, while flagging that "the devil will be in the detail." *Source: CCC responds to Government's Transport Decarbonisation Plan, Climate Change Committee, 14 July 2021. https://www.theccc.org.uk/2021/07/14/ccc-responds-to-governments-transport-decarbonisation-plan/.

The CCC's 2025 assessment confirmed that "61% of the required reduction in emissions to hit the 2030 NDC is covered by either credible plans or has some associated risks," while "for the remaining 39%, there are either significant risks, or insufficient or unquantified plans." *Source: Progress in Reducing Emissions — 2025 Report to Parliament, Climate Change Committee, 25 June 2025. https://www.theccc.org.uk/publication/progress-in-reducing-emissions-2025-report-to-parliament.

*Analytical inference:* The CCC's 39% unquantified-plan gap is a regulatory-velocity signal: where delivery is behind, the government faces pressure to accelerate rulemaking. Surface transport's outsized 30% share of required 2030 savings makes it the most likely candidate for tightened enforcement or additional legislative instruments in the near term.

Innovate UK is the delivery partner for the ZEHID programme, operating in partnership with DfT. "The ZEHID programme, backed by circa £200m in funding from the Department for Transport and delivered in partnership with Innovate UK, is rising to meet this challenge." *Source: Zero Emission Heavy Goods Vehicles and Infrastructure, Innovate UK Business Connect. https://iuk-business-connect.org.uk/programme/zero-emission-heavy-goods-vehicles-and-infrastructure/.

Named programme participants include Project Electric Freightway, eFREIGHT 2030 (a consortium of electric HGV manufacturers, fleet operators, charging infrastructure providers, and data analysts), Zero Emission National (ZEN) Freight (demonstrating both battery-electric and hydrogen fuel cell HGVs in parallel), and HyHAUL (2024–2025, hydrogen supply chain and refuelling infrastructure). *Source: Zero Emission Heavy Goods Vehicles and Infrastructure, Innovate UK Business Connect. https://iuk-business-connect.org.uk/programme/zero-emission-heavy-goods-vehicles-and-infrastructure/.

Independent evaluation is being conducted by Ricardo, with findings described as "both public and fed to the Department for Transport." *Source: Zero Emission Heavy Goods Vehicles and Infrastructure, Innovate UK Business Connect. https://iuk-business-connect.org.uk/programme/zero-emission-heavy-goods-vehicles-and-infrastructure/.

---

# ZEHID Programme: In-Progress Intelligence (Forward-Intelligence Priority)

MONITORING — Re-check: Q4 2026

This section surfaces ZEHID as first-class forward intelligence, not background context. The programme is the primary live delivery vehicle of the TDP's freight decarbonisation commitments and is generating real-world data that will shape future regulation and procurement conditions for road freight operators across UK lanes.

**Programme scope and scale.** ZEHID aims "to deploy around 350 of the heaviest battery-electric and hydrogen fuel cell vehicles on UK roads" and "fund and support over 70 public and depot-based infrastructure installations by 2030." *Source: Zero Emission Heavy Goods Vehicles and Infrastructure, Innovate UK Business Connect. https://iuk-business-connect.org.uk/programme/zero-emission-heavy-goods-vehicles-and-infrastructure/.

**Phase 1 — Procurement and Infrastructure Development: 2024–2026.** This phase is described as "building the foundation with critical charging and refuelling infrastructure." *Source: Zero Emission Heavy Goods Vehicles and Infrastructure, Innovate UK Business Connect. https://iuk-business-connect.org.uk/programme/zero-emission-heavy-goods-vehicles-and-infrastructure/.

**Phase 2 — On-Road Demonstration: 2026–2031.** "Real-world trials of zero-emission HGVs, testing their capabilities in various duty cycles and operational scenarios." *Source: Zero Emission Heavy Goods Vehicles and Infrastructure, Innovate UK Business Connect. https://iuk-business-connect.org.uk/programme/zero-emission-heavy-goods-vehicles-and-infrastructure/.

**What ZEHID is investigating.** The programme aims to "demonstrate the feasibility of battery-electric and hydrogen-powered trucks in real-world applications," provide operators and policymakers with "critical insights into the performance, infrastructure needs, and cost-effectiveness of these technologies," and "establish the fuelling and charging infrastructure needed to support widespread adoption across the UK." *Source: Zero Emission Heavy Goods Vehicles and Infrastructure, Innovate UK Business Connect. https://iuk-business-connect.org.uk/programme/zero-emission-heavy-goods-vehicles-and-infrastructure/.

**Stated technology pathways under test.** Battery-electric and hydrogen fuel cell HGVs are both in active demonstration. The ZEN Freight consortium is "conducting parallel demonstrations with both battery electric and hydrogen fuel cell heavy goods vehicles." HyHAUL (2024–2025) focused specifically on "a hydrogen supply chain and provide public access hydrogen refuelling infrastructure." *Source: Zero Emission Heavy Goods Vehicles and Infrastructure, Innovate UK Business Connect. https://iuk-business-connect.org.uk/programme/zero-emission-heavy-goods-vehicles-and-infrastructure/.

**Published outputs already available.** Hitachi ZeroCarbon has published an interim findings report covering "infrastructure and vehicle deployment, combined with insights from drivers and operators and a review of the barriers encountered during the transition to electric HGVs." A cost calculator has been deployed "to help operators understand the commercial and environmental implications of electrifying their fleets." BSI has published three Flex standards: BSI Flex 2071 v2.0 (Publicly Accessible Charging Sites for Battery Electric HGVs), BSI Flex 2072 v2.0 (Workshops for Zero-Emission Heavy-Duty Vehicles), and BSI Flex 2073 v2.0 (Design and Implementation of Hydrogen Refuelling Sites). *Source: Zero Emission Heavy Goods Vehicles and Infrastructure, Innovate UK Business Connect. https://iuk-business-connect.org.uk/programme/zero-emission-heavy-goods-vehicles-and-infrastructure/.

*Operational implication:* Workspaces operating or procuring temperature-controlled, specialist, or high-value cargo road transport across UK lanes — including live events, high-value automotive, and humanitarian freight — should engage with ZEHID interim outputs now. The Hitachi ZeroCarbon cost calculator and interim findings report are publicly available and provide carrier-level cost-of-transition data that can directly inform supplier contract negotiations and fleet transition discussions with road hauliers.

*Analytical inference:* The Ricardo independent evaluation, feeding findings to DfT, means that ZEHID Phase 2 data (2026–2031) will directly shape the policy instruments that follow the TDP — likely including sector-specific mandates or incentive structures for zero-emission heavy freight. Workspaces that track ZEHID outputs are effectively reading the regulatory pipeline two to three years ahead of formal consultation.

**What remains pending.** The full Ricardo evaluation of on-road performance is a Phase 2 output (2026–2031); no final data is yet available. Cost parity timelines between zero-emission HGVs and diesel equivalents are not stated in the source content.

*Specific cost-parity figures for battery-electric and hydrogen HGVs versus diesel equivalents not available from primary sources as of 1 July 2025.*

---

# Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**Current state.** The CCC's 2025 report confirms that surface transport emissions "are reducing for the second year in a row," signalling that policy is beginning to produce measurable results. *Source: Progress in Reducing Emissions — 2025 Report to Parliament, Climate Change Committee, 25 June 2025. https://www.theccc.org.uk/publication/progress-in-reducing-emissions-2025-report-to-parliament.

However, as of 2019, "surface transport is currently the largest emitting sector of the UK economy, making up 23% of total UK territorial emissions in 2019 or 113 MtCO₂e. Emissions from this sector have been broadly flat over the past decade, falling just 1% between 2009 and 2019. They need to fall by 90% by 2050 to meet the economy-wide Net Zero target." *Source: CCC responds to Government's Transport Decarbonisation Plan, Climate Change Committee, 14 July 2021. https://www.theccc.org.uk/2021/07/14/ccc-responds-to-governments-transport-decarbonisation-plan/.

*Analytical inference:* The gap between the historical 1% decade-long reduction and the 90%-by-2050 target is the structural pressure behind every TDP delivery vehicle. When the CCC's 2025 report flags 39% of required 2030 reductions as lacking credible plans, the probability of accelerated or additional rulemaking — particularly for heavy freight — rises materially.

**Conversion triggers — what would move this from signal to active commercial pressure:**

1. **ZEHID Phase 1 infrastructure completion (end 2026).** If the 70+ charging and refuelling installations are delivered on schedule, the infrastructure excuse for diesel retention weakens. *Analytical inference:* Post-2026, DfT has the evidence base to tighten procurement requirements on publicly funded logistics contracts.

2. **Ricardo interim evaluation publication.** The programme's public evaluation outputs are designed to inform both operators and policymakers. *Analytical inference:* Any Ricardo finding that battery-electric or hydrogen HGVs are operationally viable at scale in UK duty cycles will accelerate manufacturer investment and reduce the lead time to commercial availability — directly affecting carrier procurement options available to workspaces.

3. **2030 NDC proximity.** The CCC's finding that surface transport carries 30% of required 2030 emission savings means that the NDC commitment itself is a hard trigger. *Analytical inference:* If surface transport progress lags in 2027–2028 CCC progress reports, emergency rulemaking (accelerated mandates, procurement requirements, surcharges) becomes politically necessary. Re-check window: June 2026 CCC Progress Report to Parliament.

4. **HGV-specific mandate consultation.** The TDP committed to phasing out diesel HGV sales by 2035 (smaller) and 2040 (larger). *Analytical inference:* A formal consultation on the legislative instrument implementing this ban — not yet identified in the source content as published — is the direct conversion trigger for fleet operators and workspace carrier contracts.

*Specific consultation date or draft legislation for the HGV diesel sales ban not available from primary sources as of 1 July 2025.*

---

# Operational and Cost Implications If It Materialises

COST ALERT

**For road-primary and road-secondary cargo movements.** The workspace, in its role as freight forwarder arranging specialist road transport for high-value cargo verticals, faces two distinct cost exposure windows:

- **Carrier surcharge risk (near term, 2025–2030).** Road carriers participating in or adjacent to ZEHID demonstrations will incur capital costs for zero-emission vehicle procurement and infrastructure. *Analytical inference:* These costs are likely to be passed through as sustainability surcharges or rate uplift, particularly on specialist services (temperature control, tail-lift, air-ride suspension) used for live events, fine art, and film and TV cargo. The timing and quantum of surcharges are carrier-specific and not publicly available.

*Effect on road freight verticals requires carrier-specific rate data.*

- **Fleet availability risk (medium term, 2028–2035).** As the diesel ban dates approach, older diesel HGV stock available for specialist cargo movements — often requiring non-standard configurations — may tighten. *Analytical inference:* Carriers with specialist vehicle types (climate-controlled, bespoke fit-out for high-value freight) may face longer lead times for zero-emission equivalent procurement, potentially creating capacity constraints.

**For air-primary movements.** The CCC's 2025 signal that aviation emissions have grown to exceed electricity supply sector emissions is a regulatory-pressure indicator for UK domestic and international aviation policy. *Analytical inference:* Workspaces routing high-value or time-critical freight (fine art, humanitarian) primarily by air should expect UK-level aviation emissions policy to tighten independently of the TDP road freight track.

**HGV emissions context.** "Heavy goods vehicles are the backbone of the UK's supply chain, but they also account for a significant 20% of domestic transport CO2 emissions." *Source: Zero Emission Heavy Goods Vehicles and Infrastructure, Innovate UK Business Connect. https://iuk-business-connect.org.uk/programme/zero-emission-heavy-goods-vehicles-and-infrastructure/.

*Operational implication:* The 20% HGV share of domestic transport CO₂ is the political justification for the pace and funding level of the ZEHID programme. Workspaces whose supply chains are materially dependent on HGV sub-contractors are exposed to the fastest-moving segment of UK transport decarbonisation policy.

---

# Competitive Implications

COMPETITIVE EDGE

**Policy tracker as intelligence asset.** Innovate UK Business Connect has created "an interactive tool to map and track UK's commitments and policies to decarbonise transport," covering "48 commitments, 9 modes of transport, 35 policy papers, 542 policies." *Source: UK Transport Decarbonisation Policy Tracker, Innovate UK Business Connect. https://iuk-business-connect.org.uk/perspectives/decarbonisation-policy-tracker/.

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces that use this tracker to map their road carrier contracts and depot infrastructure against specific TDP commitments gain a policy-monitoring capability that most freight forwarders do not have. This is a directly actionable competitive edge: knowing which of the 48 commitments affect which carrier relationships before the carriers themselves have modelled the impact.

**Client-facing sustainability positioning.** The TDP defines "a sectoral net zero pathway that combines decarbonising efforts in all forms of transport, and transversal measures such as a push for hydrogen and the unlocking of green finance." *Source: Decarbonising Transport: A Better, Greener Britain, Climate Change Laws of the World (Grantham/LSE). https://climate-laws.org/document/transport-decarbonisation-plan_99f8.

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces serving clients in the live events, film and TV, and luxury sectors — where sustainability credentials are increasingly part of client procurement criteria — demonstrable engagement with the TDP's delivery vehicles (monitoring ZEHID outcomes, requiring carrier transition roadmaps from hauliers) provides credible sustainability positioning ahead of competitors who are tracking compliance dates only.

**Named competitor positioning.** No sourced reporting on specific named freight forwarding competitor positioning relative to TDP or ZEHID participation is available from the source blocks provided.

*Specific competitor activity relative to ZEHID or TDP not available from primary sources as of 1 July 2025.*

---

# Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**
- The UK government has committed £200m to demonstrating zero-emission HGVs at scale, with 350 vehicles and 70 infrastructure installations targeted by 2030. This is not an aspiration — it is a funded programme with named industry partners in live trials. *Source: ZEHID, Innovate UK Business Connect.*
- The CCC's June 2025 report confirmed surface transport is reducing emissions for the second consecutive year, while identifying it as the single largest contributor (30%) to the UK's 2030 climate target gap. Policy pressure on this sector is therefore intensifying, not easing.
- Diesel HGV sales bans are set for 2035 (smaller trucks) and 2040 (larger trucks). Road carrier fleets used for specialist cargo today will be in transition within 5–10 years; asking carriers now about their transition plans is a reasonable procurement question.

**Questions to pose to clients:**
- Does your sustainability reporting require disclosure of Scope 3 road freight emissions? If so, carrier transition timelines affect the credibility of your 2030 claims.
- Do your procurement policies include carrier sustainability requirements? The ZEHID interim findings and BSI Flex standards provide an objective basis for setting minimum criteria.
- Are you open to co-investing in route-level sustainability — for example, preferencing carriers participating in ZEHID or publishing transition roadmaps?

**Claims to avoid:**
- Do not claim the workspace's current carrier base is "compliant" with TDP requirements — the TDP is a policy framework, not a compliance instrument, and no compliance obligation falls on freight forwarders as such under the TDP.
- Do not assert specific cost figures for zero-emission HGV surcharges — these are not publicly available.
- Do not present the 2035 or 2040 diesel ban dates as enacted law without legal confirmation of the specific legislative instrument. *Legal Confirmation Required:* The enacted legal basis for the HGV diesel sales bans requires confirmation against specific enacted regulations rather than the TDP policy document.

---

# What the Workspace Should Do Now

**Action 1 — Map road carrier contracts against TDP diesel ban timelines.** For every road haulier used for UK-lane specialist cargo (live events, fine art, film and TV, automotive), identify the vehicle type (by weight class), current fleet age, and the carrier's stated transition plan if any. Flag contracts extending to 2030+ for transition-risk assessment. *Priority: complete before end of Q3 2025.*

**Action 2 — Access ZEHID public outputs.** The Hitachi ZeroCarbon interim findings report and HGV carbon and cost calculator are publicly available at https://iuk-business-connect.org.uk/programme/zero-emission-heavy-goods-vehicles-and-infrastructure/. Use the cost calculator to model the cost-of-transition range for key carrier relationships. This is free intelligence from a government-funded programme.

**Action 3 — Subscribe to ZEHID mailing list.** The programme has a mailing list for updates on vehicle and infrastructure deployment. Given that Ricardo evaluation outputs feed directly to DfT policy, early access to findings is regulatory-pipeline intelligence.

**Action 4 — Use the Innovate UK policy tracker.** Map the workspace's primary UK transport commitments (by mode and geography) against the 48 TDP commitments tracked at https://iuk-business-connect.org.uk/perspectives/decarbonisation-policy-tracker/. Identify which commitments have delivery status changes that affect the workspace's carrier or airport relationships.

**Action 5 — Add carrier transition-plan clauses to new road haulage contracts.** *Legal Confirmation Required:* The specific drafting of carrier sustainability requirements and any associated audit rights requires legal review. However, commercially, requiring carriers to provide a written zero-emission fleet transition roadmap as part of contract renewal is an immediately actionable risk-management step that does not require legal confirmation to initiate as a procurement preference.

**Action 6 — Set a monitoring calendar.** Re-check the brief at:
- June 2026: CCC Progress Report to Parliament (next annual assessment — surface transport progress against 2030 NDC will indicate whether regulatory acceleration is likely)
- Q4 2026: End of ZEHID Phase 1 (infrastructure completion milestone; Ricardo interim outputs expected)
- Any date a formal HGV diesel sales ban consultation is published by DfT

---

# Confirmed Policy Timeline

| Date | Event | Status |
|------|--------|--------|
| March 2020 | *Decarbonising Transport: Setting the Challenge* published | In force as of March 2020 |
| 14 July 2021 | TDP (*Decarbonising Transport: A Better, Greener Britain*) published | In force as of 14 July 2021 |
| July 2022 | One-year-on review published | In force as of July 2022 |
| 2024–2026 | ZEHID Phase 1: Procurement and Infrastructure Development | Active — in progress |
| 2026–2031 | ZEHID Phase 2: On-Road Demonstration with Ricardo evaluation | Scheduled — not yet commenced |
| 2035 | Ban on sale of new diesel trucks weighing up to 26 tonnes | Policy commitment — *Legal Confirmation Required* on enacted instrument |
| 2040 | Ban on sale of new large diesel trucks weighing more than 26 tonnes | Policy commitment — *Legal Confirmation Required* on enacted instrument |
| 2050 | Net Zero transport / Net Zero aviation targets | Policy commitment aligned with UK Climate Change Act obligations |

*Sources: Transport Decarbonisation Plan, DfT, 14 July 2021; CCC responds to TDP, 14 July 2021; ZEHID programme page, Innovate UK Business Connect.*

---
